subject: Mathematics
q: The value of $\operatorname{cosec} 10^{\circ}-\sqrt{3} \sec 10^{\circ}$ is equal to :
question: The value of $\operatorname{cosec} 10^{\circ}-\sqrt{3} \sec 10^{\circ}$ is equal to :
solution:
$\operatorname{cosec} 10° - \sqrt{3} \sec 10° = \frac{\cos 10° - \sqrt{3} \sin 10°}{\sin 10° \cos 10°}$.
Numerator: $\cos 10° - \sqrt{3} \sin 10° = 2\left(\frac{1}{2}\cos 10° - \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin 10°\right) = 2\cos 70° = 2\sin 20°$.
Denominator: $\sin 10° \cos 10° = \frac{1}{2}\sin 20°$.
Result $= \frac{2\sin 20°}{\frac{1}{2}\sin 20°} = 4$.

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\sin x=-\frac{3}{5}$, where $\pi < x < \frac{3 \pi}{2}$, then $80\left(\tan ^2 x-\cos x\right)$ is equal to
question: If $\sin x=-\frac{3}{5}$, where $\pi < x < \frac{3 \pi}{2}$, then $80\left(\tan ^2 x-\cos x\right)$ is equal to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \sin x=\frac{-3}{5}, \pi < x < \frac{3 \pi}{2} \\ & \tan x=\frac{3}{4} \cos x=-\frac{4}{5} \\ & 80\left(\tan ^2 x-\cos x\right) \\ & =80\left(\frac{9}{16}+\frac{4}{5}\right)=45+64=109\end{aligned}$

solution:
Given,

tan9o-tan27o-tan63o+tan81o

=cot81°+tan81o-tan27°+cot27o

=tan9°+cot9°-tan27°+cot27°

=sin9°cos9°+cos9°sin9°-sin27°cos27°+cos27°sin27°

=2sin18o-2sin54o

=2×45-1-2×45+1

=815-1-15+1

=85+1-5-15-15+1

=825-1=4

subject: Mathematics
q: If $A = \dfrac{\sin 3^\circ}{\cos 9^\circ} + \dfrac{\sin 9^\circ}{\cos 27^\circ} + \dfrac{\sin 27^\circ}{\cos 81^\circ}$ and $B = \tan 81^\circ - \tan 3^\circ$, then $\dfrac{B}{A}$ is equal to _____.
question: If $A = \dfrac{\sin 3^\circ}{\cos 9^\circ} + \dfrac{\sin 9^\circ}{\cos 27^\circ} + \dfrac{\sin 27^\circ}{\cos 81^\circ}$ and $B = \tan 81^\circ - \tan 3^\circ$, then $\dfrac{B}{A}$ is equal to _____.
solution:
Consider the general term $\dfrac{\sin x}{\cos 3x}$.

We can rewrite this by multiplying and dividing by $2\cos x$:

$\dfrac{2\sin x \cos x}{2\cos 3x \cos x} = \dfrac{\sin 2x}{2\cos 3x \cos x}$

Using $\sin 2x = \sin(3x - x) = \sin 3x \cos x - \cos 3x \sin x$, we get:

$\dfrac{\sin 3x \cos x - \cos 3x \sin x}{2\cos 3x \cos x} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{\sin 3x}{\cos 3x} - \dfrac{\sin x}{\cos x} \right) = \dfrac{1}{2} (\tan 3x - \tan x)$

Applying this identity to each term of $A$:

$\dfrac{\sin 3^\circ}{\cos 9^\circ} = \dfrac{1}{2} (\tan 9^\circ - \tan 3^\circ)$

$\dfrac{\sin 9^\circ}{\cos 27^\circ} = \dfrac{1}{2} (\tan 27^\circ - \tan 9^\circ)$

$\dfrac{\sin 27^\circ}{\cos 81^\circ} = \dfrac{1}{2} (\tan 81^\circ - \tan 27^\circ)$

Adding these terms, the series telescopes:

$A = \dfrac{1}{2} (\tan 9^\circ - \tan 3^\circ + \tan 27^\circ - \tan 9^\circ + \tan 81^\circ - \tan 27^\circ)$

$A = \dfrac{1}{2} (\tan 81^\circ - \tan 3^\circ)$

We are given $B = \tan 81^\circ - \tan 3^\circ$.

Substituting $B$ into the equation for $A$:

$A = \dfrac{1}{2} B$

$\Rightarrow \dfrac{B}{A} = 2$

Answer: $2$

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>cosec </mtext><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mtext>p</mtext><mo>+</mo><mtext>q</mtext></mrow><mrow><mtext>  p</mtext><mo>-</mo><mtext>q  </mtext></mrow></mfrac><mfenced><mrow><mtext>p</mtext><mo>≠</mo><mtext>q, p</mtext><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo></math>then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mtext>cot</mtext><mfenced><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is equals to:

solution:
cosec  θ = p + q p - q ⇒ 1 sin  θ = p + q p - q

let  tan θ 2 = t    then    1 + t 2 2t = p + q p - q  ∵sin2θ=2tanθ1+tan2θ

Using componendo and dividendo rule, we get

⇒ 1 + t 2 + 2t 1 + t 2 - 2t = p + q + p - q p + q - p + q  

⇒ 1 + t 1 - t 2 = p q

⇒  |1-t1+t|  =qp=|cot(π4+θ)| ∵tanπ4+θ=1+tanθ1-tanθ

⇒sinα+βsinα-β=23

⇒tanα=-5tanβ

⇒k=-5

question: The period of $\sin ^2 \theta$ is
solution: $\sin ^2 \theta=\frac{1-\cos 2 \theta}{2} ;$ Period $=\frac{2 \pi}{2}=\pi$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $\frac{\pi}{2}<\theta<\pi$ and $\cot \theta=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}$. Then the value of
$\sin \left(\frac{15 \theta}{2}\right)(\cos 8 \theta+\sin 8 \theta)+\cos \left(\frac{15 \theta}{2}\right)(\cos 8 \theta-\sin 8 \theta)$
is equal to
question:
Let $\frac{\pi}{2}<\theta<\pi$ and $\cot \theta=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}$. Then the value of
$\sin \left(\frac{15 \theta}{2}\right)(\cos 8 \theta+\sin 8 \theta)+\cos \left(\frac{15 \theta}{2}\right)(\cos 8 \theta-\sin 8 \theta)$
is equal to
solution:
Expanding and regrouping:
$\sin\frac{15\theta}{2}(\cos 8\theta + \sin 8\theta) + \cos\frac{15\theta}{2}(\cos 8\theta - \sin 8\theta)$
$= \left(\cos\frac{15\theta}{2}\cos 8\theta + \sin\frac{15\theta}{2}\sin 8\theta\right) + \left(\sin\frac{15\theta}{2}\cos 8\theta - \cos\frac{15\theta}{2}\sin 8\theta\right)$
$= \cos\left(8\theta - \frac{15\theta}{2}\right) + \sin\left(\frac{15\theta}{2} - 8\theta\right) = \cos\frac{\theta}{2} - \sin\frac{\theta}{2}$.
From $\cot\theta = -\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}$: $\sin\theta = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3}$, $\cos\theta = -\frac{1}{3}$.
$\left(\cos\frac{\theta}{2} - \sin\frac{\theta}{2}\right)^2 = 1 - \sin\theta = 1 - \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{3} = \frac{(\sqrt{2}-1)^2}{3}$.
Since $\frac{\pi}{4} < \frac{\theta}{2} < \frac{\pi}{2}$, $\sin\frac{\theta}{2} > \cos\frac{\theta}{2}$, so the value is $\frac{1-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}}$.

q: If <math><mn>5</mn><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">tan</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>-</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of <math><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow></math> is
question: If <math><mn>5</mn><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">tan</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>-</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of <math><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow></math> is
solution:
51-cos⁡2x1+cos⁡2x-1+cos⁡2x2=2cos⁡2x+9

Let, cos⁡2x=y

 ∴    5(1−y1+y−1+y2)=2y+9

⇒ 5-y2-4y+1=4y2+22y+18

⇒ 9y2+42y+13=0

⇒ y=-13 or y=-133 (Not possible)

Now, cos⁡4x=2cos2⁡2x-1

⇒ cos⁡4x=2-132-1

⇒ cos⁡4x=-79

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\sin x+\sin ^2 x=1, x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$, then $\left(\cos ^{12} x+\tan ^{12} x\right)+3\left(\cos ^{10} x+\tan ^{10} x+\cos ^8 x+\tan ^8 x\right)+\left(\cos ^6 x+\tan ^6 x\right)$ is equal to :
question: If $\sin x+\sin ^2 x=1, x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$, then $\left(\cos ^{12} x+\tan ^{12} x\right)+3\left(\cos ^{10} x+\tan ^{10} x+\cos ^8 x+\tan ^8 x\right)+\left(\cos ^6 x+\tan ^6 x\right)$ is equal to :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \sin x+\sin ^2 x=1 \\
& \Rightarrow \sin x=\cos ^2 x \Rightarrow \tan x=\cos x
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Given expression
$\begin{aligned}
& =2 \cos ^{12} x+6\left[\cos ^{10} x+\cos ^8 x\right]+2 \cos ^6 x \\
& =2\left[\sin ^6 x+3 \sin ^5 x+3 \sin ^4 x+\sin ^3 x\right] \\
& =2 \sin ^3 x\left[(\sin x+1)^3\right] \\
& =2\left[\sin ^2 x+\sin x\right]^3 \\
& =2
\end{aligned}$

q: The maximum value of <math><mn>3</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></math> for any real value of <math><mi>θ</mi></math> is :
question: The maximum value of <math><mn>3</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></math> for any real value of <math><mi>θ</mi></math> is :

⇒fθ=532sin⁡θ+12cos⁡θ

Now using the concept acosx+bsinx+c ∈ c-a2+b2, c+a2+b2, we can write

Maximum value of fθ is 5322+122=754+14=19

subject: Mathematics
q: The least value of $\left(\cos ^{2} \theta-6 \sin \theta \cos \theta+3 \sin ^{2} \theta+2\right)$ is
question: The least value of $\left(\cos ^{2} \theta-6 \sin \theta \cos \theta+3 \sin ^{2} \theta+2\right)$ is
solution:
$\cos^2\theta + 3\sin^2\theta - 3\sin 2\theta + 2$.
Using double angle formulas:
$= \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2} + \frac{3(1-\cos 2\theta)}{2} - 3\sin 2\theta + 2$
$= 4 - \cos 2\theta - 3\sin 2\theta$.
Minimum of $-\cos 2\theta - 3\sin 2\theta = -\sqrt{1+9} = -\sqrt{10}$.
Least value $= 4 - \sqrt{10}$.

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\cot x=\frac{5}{12}$ for some $x \in\left(\pi, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right)$, then $\sin 7 x\left(\cos \frac{13 x}{2}+\sin \frac{13 x}{2}\right)+\cos 7 x\left(\cos \frac{13 x}{2}-\sin \frac{13 x}{2}\right)$ is equal to
question: If $\cot x=\frac{5}{12}$ for some $x \in\left(\pi, \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right)$, then $\sin 7 x\left(\cos \frac{13 x}{2}+\sin \frac{13 x}{2}\right)+\cos 7 x\left(\cos \frac{13 x}{2}-\sin \frac{13 x}{2}\right)$ is equal to
solution:
$\cot x = \dfrac{5}{12} \Rightarrow \cos x = \dfrac{-5}{13} = 2\cos^2 \dfrac{x}{2} - 1$
$\cos\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right) = -\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$ or $\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}}$ (rejected)
$\left\{ \because \dfrac{x}{2} \in \left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{3\pi}{4}\right) \right\}$
$\left(\sin 7x \dfrac{\sin 13x}{2} + \cos 7x \dfrac{\cos 13x}{2}\right) + \left(\sin 7x \dfrac{\cos 13x}{2} - \cos 7x \dfrac{\sin 13x}{2}\right)$
$\cos\left(7x - \dfrac{13x}{2}\right) + \sin\left(7x - \dfrac{13x}{2}\right)$
$\cos \dfrac{x}{2} + \sin\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)$
$\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{13}} - \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{13}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{13}}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{18}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{18}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{18}\right) = K$, then the value of $\sin\left(\dfrac{10K\pi}{3}\right)$ is :
question: If $\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{18}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{18}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{18}\right) = K$, then the value of $\sin\left(\dfrac{10K\pi}{3}\right)$ is :
solution:
Given $K = \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{18}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{18}\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{7\pi}{18}\right)$

Using the standard trigonometric identity $\sin(\theta) \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3} - \theta\right) \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{3} + \theta\right) = \dfrac{1}{4} \sin(3\theta)$ with $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{18}$:

$\dfrac{\pi}{3} - \theta = \dfrac{6\pi}{18} - \dfrac{\pi}{18} = \dfrac{5\pi}{18}$

$\dfrac{\pi}{3} + \theta = \dfrac{6\pi}{18} + \dfrac{\pi}{18} = \dfrac{7\pi}{18}$

Applying the identity, we get:

$K = \dfrac{1}{4} \sin\left(3 \times \dfrac{\pi}{18}\right) = \dfrac{1}{4} \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$K = \dfrac{1}{4} \times \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{8}$

We need to find the value of $\sin\left(\dfrac{10K\pi}{3}\right)$. Substituting $K = \dfrac{1}{8}$:

$\sin\left(\dfrac{10 \times \dfrac{1}{8} \times \pi}{3}\right) = \sin\left(\dfrac{10\pi}{24}\right) = \sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right)$

Expanding $\dfrac{5\pi}{12}$ as $\dfrac{\pi}{4} + \dfrac{\pi}{6}$:

$\sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) = \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4} + \dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$\sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) = \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)\cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) + \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$\sin\left(\dfrac{5\pi}{12}\right) = \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} + 1}{2\sqrt{2}}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\sqrt{3} + 1}{2\sqrt{2}}$

q: If the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>λ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has real solutions for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> lies in interval
question: If the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>λ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> has real solutions for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math> lies in interval
solution:
-λ=sin4θ+cos4θ

=sin2θ+cos2θ2-2sin2θcos2θ

=1-4sin2θcos2θ2

=1-sin22θ2

⇒λ=sin22θ2-1

λ∈-1,-12

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $|\cos \theta \cos (60-\theta) \cos (60+\theta)| \leq \frac{1}{8}, \theta \epsilon[0,2 \pi]$. Then, the sum of all $\theta \epsilon[0,2 \pi]$, where $\cos 3 \theta$ attains its maximum value, is :
question: Let $|\cos \theta \cos (60-\theta) \cos (60+\theta)| \leq \frac{1}{8}, \theta \epsilon[0,2 \pi]$. Then, the sum of all $\theta \epsilon[0,2 \pi]$, where $\cos 3 \theta$ attains its maximum value, is :
solution:
We know that
$(\cos \theta)\left(\cos( 6 0 ^ { \circ } - \theta ) \left(\cos \left(60^{\circ}+\theta\right)=\frac{1}{4} \cos 3 \theta\right.\right.$
So equation reduces to $\left|\frac{1}{4} \cos 3 \theta\right| \leq \frac{1}{8}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow|\cos 3 \theta| \leq \frac{1}{2} \\
& \Rightarrow-\frac{1}{2} \leq \cos 3 \theta \leq \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow$ maximum value of $\cos 3 \theta=\frac{1}{2}$, here
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 3 \theta=2 \mathrm{n} \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{3} \\
& \theta=\frac{2 \mathrm{n} \pi}{3} \pm \frac{\pi}{9}
\end{aligned}$
As $\theta \in[0,2 \pi]$ possible values are
$\theta=\left\{\frac{\pi}{9}, \frac{5 \pi}{9}, \frac{7 \pi}{9}, \frac{11 \pi}{9}, \frac{13 \pi}{9}, \frac{17 \pi}{9}\right\}$
Whose sum is
$\frac{\pi}{9}+\frac{5 \pi}{9}+\frac{7 \pi}{9}+\frac{11 \pi}{9}+\frac{13 \pi}{9}+\frac{17 \pi}{9}=\frac{54 \pi}{9}=6 \pi$

q: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mn>9</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mn>27</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mn>81</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mn>243</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mn>9</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mn>27</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mn>81</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mn>243</mn><mo>∘</mo></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
We need to find the value of

364 cos2 9∘-1 4 cos2 27∘-1 4 cos2 81∘-1 4 cos2 243∘-1 

=364 cos29∘-1 4 sin29∘-1 4 cos227∘-14 sin227∘-1

=3642cos9∘sin9°2-4cos29°-4sin29°+1 42sin27∘cos9°2-4cos227°-4sin227°+1

=364 sin218∘-4 +1 4 sin254∘-4+1

=364 sin218∘-3 4 sin254∘-3
=365-124-3 5+124-3
=366-25-1246+25-124
=-36252-3616=36

Hence this is the correct option.

q: In a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math>, and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>∠</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> Then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>∠</mi><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>∠</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to:
question: In a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math>, and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>∠</mi><mi>C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mo>.</mo></math> Then the ordered pair <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>∠</mi><mi>A</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>∠</mi><mi>B</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to:
solution:
ab>1⇒∠A>∠B

A+B=120°

⇒sin(120°−B)sinB=2+3

⇒  32cotB+12=2+3

⇒ cotB=2332+3

 ⇒cotB=2+3

⇒   B=15°

Hence, (∠A,∠B)=(105°,15°)

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>9</mn></munderover><mi>sec</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>6</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mi>sec</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>6</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>8</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><munderover><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>9</mn></munderover><mi>sec</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>6</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mi>sec</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>6</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>8</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then
solution:
Let θ+m-1π6=x and θ+mπ6=y

So, y-x=π6

Now, ∑m=19secθ+m-1π6secθ+mπ6

=∑m=19secxsecy=∑m=191cosxcosy

=2∑m=19siny-xcosxcosy=2∑m=19tany-tanx

=2∑m=19tanθ+mπ6-tanθ+(m-1)π6

=2tanθ+π6-tanθ+0·π6+2tanθ+2π6-tanθ+π6

+2tanθ+3π6-tanθ+2π6+ ... +2tanθ+9π6-tanθ+8π6

=2tanθ+9π6-tanθ=2-cotθ-tanθ

i.e. 2-cotθ-tanθ=-83 (Given)

∴  tanθ+cotθ=43

⇒tanθ=13 or 3 (as θ∈0,π2)

So, S=π6,π3

Hence, ∑θ∈Sθ=π6+π3=π2

q: For any <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow><mn>6</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></math> equals:
question: For any <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> the expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>6</mn><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow><mn>6</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></math> equals:
solution:
3sinθ-cosθ4+6cosθ+sinθ2+4sin6θ

=3sinθ-cosθ22+6cosθ+sinθ2+4sin6θ

=3cos2θ+sin2θ-2sinθcosθ2+6cos2θ+sin2θ+2sinθcosθ+4sin6θ

=31-2sinθcosθ2+61+2sinθcosθ+4sin6θ

=31-4sinθcosθ+4sin2θcos2θ+61+2sinθcosθ+4sin6θ

=9+12sin2θcos2θ+4sin6θ

=9+121-cos2θcos2θ+41-cos2θ3

=9+12cos2θ-12cos4θ+41-3cos2θ+3cos4θ-cos6θ

=13-4cos6θ

q: Let $\alpha, \beta$ be such that $\pi < \alpha-\beta < 3 \pi$. If $\sin \alpha+\sin \beta=-\frac{21}{65}$ and $\cos \alpha+\cos \beta=-\frac{27}{65}$, then the value of $\cos \frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}$ is
question: Let $\alpha, \beta$ be such that $\pi < \alpha-\beta < 3 \pi$. If $\sin \alpha+\sin \beta=-\frac{21}{65}$ and $\cos \alpha+\cos \beta=-\frac{27}{65}$, then the value of $\cos \frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}$ is
solution:
$\sin \alpha+\sin \beta=-\frac{21}{65}$ and $\cos \alpha+\cos \beta=-\frac{27}{65}$.
Squaring and adding, we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2+2 \cos (\alpha-\beta)=\frac{1170}{(65)^2} \\
& \Rightarrow \cos ^2\left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)=\frac{9}{130} \Rightarrow \cos \left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)=\frac{-3}{\sqrt{130}} \quad\left(\because \frac{\pi}{2} < \frac{\alpha-\beta}{2} < \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right) .
\end{aligned}
$$

q: If <math><mi>m</mi></math> and <math><mi>M</mi></math> are the minimum and the maximum values of <math><mn>4</mn><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>x</mi><mi> </mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math><mi>M</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>m</mi></math> is equal to:
question: If <math><mi>m</mi></math> and <math><mi>M</mi></math> are the minimum and the maximum values of <math><mn>4</mn><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mi>x</mi><mi> </mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math><mi>M</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>m</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
Given expression is 4+12sin2⁡2x-2cos4⁡x

=  4+21-cos2⁡xcos2⁡x-2cos4⁡x

Creating perfect squares, we get

=-4 cos2⁡x-142-1716 

Since, 0 ≤cos2⁡x ≤1

⇒ -14 ≤cos2⁡x-14 ≤34

⇒ 0 ≤cos2⁡x-142 ≤916

⇒ -1716 ≤cos2⁡x-142-1716 ≤916-1716

⇒ 174 ≥-4 cos2⁡x-142-1716 ≥2

⇒ M=174 and m=2
⇒ M-m=174-2=94

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>96</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>33</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>33</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>33</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>8</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>33</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>16</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>33</mn></mfrac></math> is equal to
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>96</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>33</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>33</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>33</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>8</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>33</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>16</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>33</mn></mfrac></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

Expression 96·cosπ33·cos2π33·cos4π33.........cos16π33

Now we know that,

cosA·cos2A·cos22A·cos23A.....·cos2n-1A=sin2nA2nsinA

Now using the above formula in given expression we get,

96·cosπ33·cos2π33·cos4π33.........cos16π33

=96×sin32π3325sinπ33

=96×sinπ-π3325sinπ33

=96×sinπ3325sinπ33 as sinπ-α=sinα

=96×132=3

subject: Mathematics
q: The value of $\left(\sin 70^{\circ}\right)\left(\cot 10^{\circ} \cot 70^{\circ}-1\right)$ is
question: The value of $\left(\sin 70^{\circ}\right)\left(\cot 10^{\circ} \cot 70^{\circ}-1\right)$ is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \left(\sin 70^{\circ}\right)\left(\cot 10^{\circ} \cot 70^{\circ}-1\right) \\ & =\sin 70^{\circ} \cot 10^{\circ} \cot 70^{\circ}-\sin 70^{\circ} \\ & =\cot 10^{\circ} \cos 70^{\circ}-\sin 70^{\circ} \\ & =\frac{\cos 10^{\circ} \cos 70^{\circ}-\sin 70^{\circ} \sin 10^{\circ}}{\sin 10^{\circ}} \\ & =\frac{\cos \left(10^{\circ}+70^{\circ}\right)}{\sin 10^{\circ}} \\ & =\frac{\cos 80^{\circ}}{\sin 10^{\circ}}=1\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\frac{\pi}{2} \leq x \leq \frac{3 \pi}{4}$, then $\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{12}{13} \cos x+\frac{5}{13} \sin x\right)$ is equal to
question: If $\frac{\pi}{2} \leq x \leq \frac{3 \pi}{4}$, then $\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{12}{13} \cos x+\frac{5}{13} \sin x\right)$ is equal to
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{12}{13} \cos x+\frac{5}{13} \sin x \\ & \text { Let } \tan \alpha=\frac{5}{12}, \alpha \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \alpha=\frac{5}{13}, \cos \alpha=\frac{12}{13}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{12}{13} \cos x+\frac{5}{13} \sin x=\cos \alpha \cos x+\sin \alpha \sin x \\ & \quad=\cos (x-\alpha) \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^{-1}[\cos (x-\alpha)]=x-\alpha \\ & \quad=x-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{12}\right)\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>sin</mi><mn>36</mn><mo>°</mo></math> is a root of which of the following equation
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>sin</mi><mn>36</mn><mo>°</mo></math> is a root of which of the following equation
solution:
We know sin36°=10-254

Let x=10-254

On squaring both sides, we get

4x2=10-25⇒16x2=10-25 

Again squaring, we get 

52=5-8x22 ⇒5=25+64x4-80x2

⇒16x4-20x2+5=0

subject: Mathematics
q: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>50</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>70</mn><mo>°</mo></math> is:
question: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>50</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mn>70</mn><mo>°</mo></math> is:
solution:
sin10°sin30°sin50°sin70°

=sin10°×1212×2sin50°sin70°

=14sin10°2sin50°sin70°

Using 2sinAsinB=cosA-B-cosA+B

=14sin10°cos20°-cos120°

=14sin10°cos20°-cos90°+30°

=14sin10°cos20°+sin30°

=14sin10°cos20°+12

=182sin10°cos20°+sin10°

Again, using 2sinAcosB=sinA+B-sinA-B 

=18sin30°-sin10°+sin10°

=1812=116.
